text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Nair  
Riverside Health Centre  
18 Market Street, London SE15 3AB  

Dear Dr Priya Nair,  

Samuel Carter, DOB: 14-03-1988, Hospital No: H736209 NHS No. 9053312247  
Flat 2B, 12 Brook Lane, London E2 7JP

Thank you for asking me to review this patient in clinic. He attended with his mother and confirmed that he currently splits each week between two households (his partner’s flat and his mother’s home). He keeps a full supply of medication at both locations and uses a shared checklist on his phone to reduce missed doses when moving between addresses. He reports setting separate morning and evening reminders and has asked both households to keep blister packs clearly labelled with dates.

He still has focal aware seizures 2 to 3 times per day, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic-clonic seizures 2 times per month, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change. He describes the focal aware events as brief episodes of rising discomfort in the abdomen with a metallic taste and preserved awareness, typically clustering around late afternoon on workdays. The drop attacks tend to occur in flurries over 24–48 hours once or twice a month, most often when sleep has been irregular after moving between homes. The bilateral convulsive events are usually nocturnal according to his partner; recovery is within several hours though he experiences headache and myalgia the following day. There have been no recent injuries, but he did report two near-falls in the shower; both bathrooms now have non-slip mats and a seated shower stool as agreed within the family. He has not needed emergency attendance this year and there have been no reported prolonged events.

He states adherence is good and that he has not intentionally missed doses. He uses the same pharmacy for both addresses and synchronises repeats to minimise gaps. He denies alcohol or recreational drug use and drinks one coffee in the morning only. Sleep can be fragmented on the first night after switching households; he is attempting a consistent bedtime routine and setting earlier transfer times to avoid late arrivals. There is no clear catamenial factor and no intercurrent illness reported. He has informed his employer of ongoing seizures and works reduced hours from home on days following nocturnal convulsions.

Neurological examination today was unremarkable. Cardiovascular and general examination were within normal limits. There were no tongue bites or abrasions on inspection. Mood appeared stable; he reports mild anticipatory anxiety around travel days but no persistent low mood, self-harm thoughts, or psychotic symptoms. Cognition in casual conversation was fluent with intact attention.

For safety, he is not driving and is aware of DVLA obligations. He and his family understand first-aid measures and have a clear plan for prolonged events should they occur. He would be willing to share seizure diaries from both households if helpful for correlation with sleep and transfer days. At present he prefers to continue current arrangements while we collate further data from his diaries and pharmacy refill records. I will also obtain prior records from your practice to document historical medication trials and any prior imaging or EEG for completeness.

I will review him again in three months or sooner if the pattern changes, if injuries occur, or if there are concerns from either household. In the meantime he will continue using a single pharmacy, duplicate pill organisers at each address, and phone-based reminders, and he will send monthly summaries of seizure counts and any triggers noted. We discussed SUDEP risk in general terms and the importance of supervision overnight when convulsions are more likely; both households are aware and have placed soft-call alarms by the bed.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: He still has focal aware seizures 2 to 3 times per day, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic-clonic seizures 2 times per month
He still has focal aware seizures 2 to 3 times per day, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic-clonic seizures 2 times per month, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit seizure frequencies mentioned: The letter states: 'focal aware seizures 2 to 3 per day', 'drop attacks occurring in batches' (frequency described as once or twice a month with flurries over 24–48 hours, but no numeric per-cluster count), and 'tonic-clonic seizures 2 times per month'. According to the rule to select the highest frequency across multiple types, compare: 2 to 3 per day (focal aware) vs drop attacks in clusters (insufficient numeric per-cluster count) vs 2 per month (tonic-clonic). The highest frequency is the focal aware seizures at 2 to 3 per day. Therefore, the final normalized label is '2 to 3 per day'.
seizure_frequency_number: He still has focal aware seizures 2 to 3 times per day, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic-clonic seizures 2 times per month
